title: SwiftUI Non-Renderer Parity Audit
summary: Current-state parity inventory and implementation roadmap for the non-renderer SwiftUI compatibility stack.
baseline: Full public SwiftUI API (validated against the local SwiftUI SDK symbol graph when available)

excluded_modules.name: Sources/OmniUINotcursesRenderer
excluded_modules.reason: Renderer behavior is explicitly out of scope for this audit.

excluded_modules.name: Sources/OmniKit
excluded_modules.reason: General utility module, not part of the SwiftUI compatibility surface.

taxonomy.supported: API or behavior exists with meaningful semantics and no known category-level gaps.
taxonomy.partial: The category is present, but important APIs are missing or implemented as approximations.
taxonomy.compile-only: The surface mainly keeps call sites compiling; behavior is passthrough, placeholder, or intentionally lightweight.
taxonomy.missing: The category is not present in the non-renderer stack.
taxonomy.unknown: The category has not been audited yet.

domains.title: App / Scene / Commands
domains.summary: App, Scene, and Commands now compose into a concrete OmniUI/notcurses root, with lightweight command-bar and preferred-size metadata support instead of pure compile-time shims.
implemented_examples: `App`, `Scene`, `WindowGroup`, `Settings`, `CommandGroup`, `SidebarCommands`, `DocumentGroup`, `MenuBarExtra`, and related app/window compile-surface names now exist in the non-renderer stack.
`SwiftUICompatibilityHarnessApp` exercises `App`, `WindowGroup`, and `.commands { ... }`, while the generated shim catalog covers much more of the app-scene name surface.
compile_only_examples: `App.main()` in `OmniUI` now launches the notcurses runner, but lifecycle/window ownership still remains far lighter than Apple SwiftUI platforms.
`defaultSize(...)` is preserved as preferred-size metadata and command groups are rendered as a lightweight bottom command bar rather than native platform menus.

patterns: public func _sceneRootView<S: Scene>(_ scene: S) -> AnyView?
public func _sceneCommandsView<S: Scene>(_ scene: S) -> AnyView?
evidence.notes: Core scene metadata and command composition now feed the portable renderer instead of being discarded.
Compatibility harness exercises the public app/scene surface.
The OmniUI-facing App entry point now launches the notcurses renderer using extracted scene root/commands metadata.

domains.title: State / Observation / Environment
domains.summary: Runtime-backed property wrappers and environment access exist, but observation is simplified and the environment surface is selective rather than SwiftUI-complete.
implemented_examples: `State`, `Binding`, `Environment`, `EnvironmentObject`, `ObservedObject`, `StateObject`, `Bindable`, `FocusState`, `AppStorage`, `Namespace`, and many focused/default-app-storage compile-surface names are present.
Dismiss/openURL environment actions exist and are used by navigation and keyboard shortcut tests.
compile_only_examples: The `@Observable` compatibility macro only adds `ObservableObject` conformance rather than full Observation tracking.
Observation updates rely on the runtime rebuild model instead of SwiftUI's native dependency graph.
missing_examples: Projected wrapper symbols such as `$document`, `$isExpanded`, `$isOn`, and `$selection`
Semantic focus/value propagation parity beyond compile surface

evidence.notes: State and binding are runtime-backed rather than compiler-special-cased.
Environment values include a focused subset of common SwiftUI keys.
Object wrappers exist without Combine or Observation runtime integration.
Common property wrappers are available for compatibility-heavy call sites.
Focus state is modeled explicitly in the portable runtime.
The compatibility module re-exports the `@Observable` macro surface.
Macro expansion is intentionally minimal.
Harness exercises the public wrapper surface.

domains.title: Core Views / Controls
domains.summary: The non-renderer stack covers many high-traffic view and control types, but some controls remain stubs or simplified approximations.
implemented_examples: `Text`, `Image`, `Button`, `Toggle`, `TextField`, `SecureField`, `Picker`, `Menu`, `Label`, `GroupBox`, `LabeledContent`, `ContentUnavailableView`, `ShareLink`, `ProgressView`, `GeometryReader`, `Canvas`, `DatePicker`, `Gauge`, `AsyncImage`, and `TimelineView` are present.

`SwiftUICompatibilityHarness` exercises `Toggle`, `TextField`, `Menu`, `GroupBox`, `LabeledContent`, `ContentUnavailableView`, `ShareLink`, and `ColorPicker`, while `OmniUICoreTests` now cover the newly added core parity views.
compile_only_examples: `ColorPicker` is explicitly described as a minimal stub for SwiftUI compatibility.
`quickLookPreview(_:)` is a passthrough compatibility modifier rather than a full Quick Look integration.

patterns: public struct Menu<Content: View, Label: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
public struct GroupBox<Label: View, Content: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
public struct Gauge<Label: View, CurrentValueLabel: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
evidence.notes: A broad set of primitive views and controls are implemented in a portable way, including the newer date/gauge/async/timeline surfaces.

patterns: // Terminal-friendly ColorPicker approximation for SwiftUI compatibility.
evidence.notes: ColorPicker now exposes a concrete terminal approximation that cycles a palette through a focusable control.
Harness covers representative control types from the public API surface.
Focused tests cover interactive controls plus the newly added core parity views.

domains.title: Collections / Data-driven Containers
domains.summary: Lists, forms, grids, tables, and data-driven repetition are available, but identity handling and richer collection APIs are still simplified.
implemented_examples: `List`, `Form`, `ForEach`, `LazyVGrid`, `Table`, `Section`, and `ScrollViewReader` exist in the non-renderer stack.
The current test suite covers list rendering, list interaction, on-delete edit mode, and model-backed queries.
compile_only_examples: `Table` is currently implemented as a list-backed table approximation rather than a native multi-column SwiftUI table.
`ForEach` intentionally does not incorporate `id` into the runtime path, so state can move when data reorders.

patterns: public struct ForEach<Data: RandomAccessCollection, ID: Hashable, Content: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
public struct Table<Data: RandomAccessCollection, ID: Hashable, RowContent: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
state may "move" if data is reordered
evidence.notes: Collections are present, with table currently funneled through the richer list implementation rather than a separate native table widget.
Existing tests cover row rendering, nested interaction, and delete support.

domains.title: Navigation / Presentation
domains.summary: Navigation stacks, split views, tabs, alerts, and sheets are implemented with real runtime behavior, but the long-tail navigation and presentation APIs remain absent.
implemented_examples: `NavigationView`, `NavigationViewStyle`, `NavigationStack`, `NavigationPath`, `NavigationLink`, `NavigationLink(value:)`, `NavigationSplitView`, `TabView`, `.tabItem`, `.sheet`, `.sheet(item:)`, `.popover(isPresented:...)`, `.popover(item:...)`, `.fullScreenCover`, `.alert`, `.confirmationDialog`, and `.navigationDestination(...)` are present.

The test suite covers adaptive split-view behavior, item-sheet presentation, popovers, confirmation-dialog action capture, and the bool/item/value navigation-destination flows; additional compile-surface names are supplied by generated shims.
compile_only_examples: `NavigationLink` outside a `NavigationStack` degrades to an inert button to preserve call-site compatibility.
Presentation modifiers such as detents and drag indicators exist but remain passthrough wrappers.

patterns: public init<V: Hashable>(value: V, @ViewBuilder label: () -> Label) where Destination == EmptyView
public struct NavigationSplitView<Sidebar: View, Detail: View>: View, _PrimitiveView
evidence.notes: Core navigation containers now include `NavigationView`, a lightweight `NavigationPath`, and value-based links.

patterns: func sheet<Item: Identifiable, Content: View>(item: Binding<Item?>, onDismiss: (() -> Void)? = nil, @ViewBuilder content: @escaping (Item) -> Content)
func popover<Item: Identifiable, Content: View>(item: Binding<Item?>, attachmentAnchor: Any = (), arrowEdge: Edge = .top, @ViewBuilder content: @escaping (Item) -> Content)
func fullScreenCover<Content: View>(isPresented: Binding<Bool>, onDismiss: (() -> Void)? = nil, @ViewBuilder content: () -> Content)
func confirmationDialog<Data, Actions: View>(_ title: String, isPresented: Binding<Bool>, titleVisibility: Visibility = .automatic, presenting data: Data, @ViewBuilder actions: (Data) -> Actions)
func navigationDestination<Value: Hashable, Destination: View>(for value: Value.Type, @ViewBuilder destination: @escaping (Value) -> Destination)
func navigationDestination<Destination: View>(isPresented: Binding<Bool>, @ViewBuilder destination: () -> Destination)
func navigationDestination<Item: Identifiable, Destination: View>(item: Binding<Item?>, @ViewBuilder destination: @escaping (Item) -> Destination)
func presentationDetents(_ detents: Set<PresentationDetent>) -> some View
evidence.notes: Overlay/presentation APIs now include item popovers, presented confirmation dialogs, and all three navigation-destination families; detents remain passthrough-only polish.
Focused tests cover split-view adaptation plus the bool/item/value navigation flows and the newer presentation overlays.
Harness exercises toolbar-backed presentation flows.

patterns: public struct DefaultNavigationViewStyle: NavigationViewStyle, Hashable, Sendable { public init() {} }
public struct ColumnNavigationViewStyle: NavigationViewStyle, Hashable, Sendable { public init() {} }
public struct DoubleColumnNavigationViewStyle: NavigationViewStyle, Hashable, Sendable { public init() {} }
evidence.notes: The compatibility layer now exposes the core `NavigationViewStyle` names used by older SwiftUI code.

domains.title: Layout / Interaction Modifiers
domains.summary: The stack/layout core and a growing subset of lifecycle and interaction modifiers are implemented, including terminal-backed search, refresh, disabled-state, help, and exit-command behavior.
implemented_examples: `HStack`, `VStack`, `ZStack`, `Group`, `.frame(...)`, `.padding(...)`, `.onTapGesture`, `.onAppear`, `.onDisappear`, `.onChange`, `.task`, `.keyboardShortcut`, `.focused(...)`, `.safeAreaInset(...)`, `.searchable`, `.refreshable`, `.disabled`, `.help`, and `.onExitCommand` now have concrete OmniUI/notcurses behavior.
The current test suite covers tap gestures, keyboard shortcuts, tasks, on-appear lifecycle, search-field editing, refresh actions, disabled controls, Quick Look overlays, and exit-command dispatch.
compile_only_examples: Several platform-specific modifiers still lower to `_Passthrough`, including `.onHover`, most text-input platform configuration modifiers, and many gesture/animation compatibility overloads.
Multi-tap gesture counts are accepted for source compatibility but not behaviorally modeled.
missing_examples: Gesture/drag semantic parity behind the generated compile-surface names

patterns: func onChange<V: Equatable>(of value: V, perform action: @escaping (_ newValue: V) -> Void)
func task(priority: Any? = nil, _ action: @escaping () async -> Void)
func refreshable(action: @escaping () async -> Void) -> some View
func disabled(_ disabled: Bool) -> some View
func help(_ text: String) -> some View
func safeAreaInset<Content: View>(edge: Edge, @ViewBuilder content: () -> Content)
evidence.notes: This file now separates concrete terminal-backed modifiers from the remaining passthrough-only platform shims.
Focused tests now cover representative lifecycle, search, refresh, disabled-state, and exit-command behavior.

domains.title: Drawing / Shapes / Animation
domains.summary: Shape and gradient APIs now emit real render-tree operations for OmniUI/notcurses, while animation-heavy SwiftUI semantics still remain simplified or compile-surface only.
implemented_examples: `Path`, `Rectangle`, `RoundedRectangle`, `Circle`, `Ellipse`, `Capsule`, `Canvas`, `Gradient`, `LinearGradient`, `RadialGradient`, `Animation`, `AnimationTimelineSchedule`, `NavigationTransition`, `ContentTransition`, many matched/symbol-effect compile-surface names, `withAnimation`, and `.phaseAnimator(...)` are present.
Canvas now records filled shapes into the render tree, and the gradient views now emit renderer-visible nodes rather than no-op placeholders.
compile_only_examples: Arbitrary shape styles are still collapsed to simplified fills, and animation/transition types largely remain terminal-friendly approximations rather than full SwiftUI semantics.
High-fidelity animation timing, matched-transition behavior, and mesh-gradient semantics are still deferred.

evidence.notes: Drawing APIs now lower to concrete gradient/shape nodes that the render tree and notcurses renderer consume.

patterns: // Minimal shape API surface for SwiftUI compatibility.
public struct Path: Hashable, Sendable, Shape, _PrimitiveView
evidence.notes: Shapes lower into the render tree, but style fidelity is still intentionally limited.

patterns: public struct AnimationTimelineSchedule: Hashable, Sendable { public init() {} }
public func withAnimation<T>(_ animation: Animation? = nil, _ body: () -> T) -> T
evidence.notes: Animation surface now also exposes an `AnimationTimelineSchedule` shim alongside lightweight animation hooks.
Harness covers the currently implemented public drawing and animation entry points.
Transition shims now exist as public drawing/navigation-adjacent surface types.

patterns: func navigationTransition(_ transition: NavigationTransition) -> some View {
func contentTransition(_ transition: ContentTransition) -> some View {
evidence.notes: Transition modifiers now have terminal-friendly approximations instead of pure passthrough shims.

domains.title: Styles / Materials / Platform Bridges
domains.summary: Style protocols still lean compile-surface, but many style modifiers, glass effects, and representable bridges now produce concrete terminal-visible approximations instead of being ignored.
implemented_examples: `ButtonStyle`, `ToggleStyle`, `PickerStyle`, `TextFieldStyle`, `DatePickerStyle`, `GaugeStyle`, `ListStyle`, `FormStyle`, `LabelStyle`, `NavigationViewStyle`, `Material`, `GlassEffect`, platform color bridges, representable protocols, and many style-related compile-surface modifiers are present.
`Color(uiColor:)` and `UIColor(Color)` bridging are available through lightweight cross-platform stand-ins.
compile_only_examples: Many style protocol bodies still collapse to simplified terminal renderings rather than full SwiftUI configuration semantics.
Platform bridges remain placeholder approximations rather than true AppKit/UIKit embedding.
missing_examples: Representable runtime integration beyond compile surface

patterns: // These are primarily compile-surface shims so third-party SwiftUI code can build.
evidence.notes: Style protocols are explicitly called out as compile-surface shims.

patterns: We provide lightweight stubs when UIKit/AppKit are unavailable so cross-platform sources compile.
public final class UIColor: NSObject, NSSecureCoding, @unchecked Sendable
evidence.notes: Platform color bridges are compile-friendly rather than semantically complete.

patterns: public protocol NSViewRepresentable: View where Body == Never
public protocol UIViewRepresentable: View where Body == Never
evidence.notes: Representable protocols now render placeholder type-name nodes instead of disappearing entirely.

patterns: // Terminal-friendly approximations for iOS/macOS 26 "Liquid Glass" APIs.
evidence.notes: The public Liquid Glass types still exist, while the modifier path now maps them to terminal-friendly background/shadow approximations.

domains.title: SwiftData Compatibility
domains.summary: The SwiftData shim provides a useful in-memory subset for model containers, contexts, and queries, but it intentionally stops far short of full persistence and schema semantics.
implemented_examples: `SwiftData` re-exports `Schema`, `ModelConfiguration`, `ModelContainer`, `ModelContext`, `Query`, and `SortOrder`.
`ModelContainer`, `ModelContext`, and `Query` work together in the in-memory compatibility layer and are covered by tests and the harness.
compile_only_examples: The top-level `SwiftData` module explicitly states that it exists so common call sites compile on non-Apple platforms.
Persistence, migrations, and advanced model metadata are intentionally out of scope for the current shim.

evidence.notes: The public SwiftData shim is deliberately small and forwarding.
The compatibility layer implements an in-memory data model.
Harness uses the public SwiftData compatibility surface end to end.
The current test suite covers query refresh and model-container stability.

domains.title: Compatibility Macros
domains.summary: The public `SwiftUI` and `SwiftData` modules expose the key macro names needed by portability-focused code, but the expansions are intentionally minimal.
implemented_examples: `#Preview`, `@Observable`, and `@Model` are available through the compatibility modules.
`SwiftUICompatibilityHarness` uses both `@Observable` and `@Model`.
compile_only_examples: `#Preview` expands to nothing so preview blocks parse and are ignored.
`@Observable` only adds `ObservableObject` conformance, and `@Model` only adds `Identifiable` conformance.
missing_examples: Generated preview providers or preview metadata

patterns: public macro Preview(_ name: String? = nil, @ViewBuilder _ body: () -> AnyView)
evidence.notes: The public SwiftUI compatibility module exposes the expected macro names.
Preview blocks are intentionally ignored after parsing.
Observable compatibility is intentionally restricted to `ObservableObject` conformance.
SwiftData models gain just enough synthesis for common list/query call sites.

roadmap.title: Surface Correctness
goals: Turn the highest-risk silent passthroughs into explicit, documented compatibility behavior.
Close the most painful public signature gaps blocking compile-time portability in the app/scene, style, SwiftData, and macro surfaces.
Keep hard-cutover behavior: prefer clear support or explicit deferral over backward-compat wrappers.
acceptance: Representative scene, command, style, macro, and SwiftData compile samples build through the compatibility modules.
The parity manifest cleanly distinguishes implemented behavior from compile-only shims in these domains.

roadmap.title: Semantic Correctness
goals: Strengthen state identity, focus, environment propagation, and lifecycle behavior.
Improve list/table/grid selection and deletion semantics, then tighten navigation and presentation behavior.
Replace the most misleading passthrough interaction modifiers with real semantics or explicit deferrals.
acceptance: Focused `OmniUICoreTests` cover state stability, navigation push/pop, tab selection, modifier lifecycle, and collection updates.
The compatibility harness can exercise these flows without depending on renderer-specific behavior.

roadmap.title: Visual and Interaction Fidelity
goals: Replace placeholder drawing and style behavior with node-tree semantics that can be consumed consistently by non-renderer runtimes.
Connect style protocols and materials to meaningful render-tree state instead of pure passthrough wrappers.
Improve control and layout modifiers whose current behavior is visually misleading compared with SwiftUI.
acceptance: Shapes, gradients, materials, and style-driven controls no longer degrade to obvious placeholders in the debug/runtime path.
Animation-related modifiers either change runtime behavior meaningfully or are explicitly documented as deferred.

roadmap.title: Public Completeness and Long Tail
goals: Continue pushing toward the full public SwiftUI baseline, or explicitly defer Apple-only APIs so the audit never goes stale.
Refresh the manifest and generated report after each implementation slice so support levels remain decision-useful.
Keep parity work balanced across domains instead of optimizing only for the shortest migration path.
acceptance: Every tracked domain has an up-to-date missing list and a clear support classification.
The generated Markdown report stays in sync with the manifest and checker output.
